Title: A three enzyme system to generate the Strychnos alkaloid scaffold from a central biosynthetic intermediate

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Akuammicine is a Strychnos alkaloid and κ-opioid receptor agonist; preakuammicine-derived alkaloids include anticancer agents vinblastine and vincristine.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: leaf epidermis-enriched tissues for pathway transcripts; exact akuammicine accumulation organ not explicitly stated
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provides strictosidine aglycone substrate required upstream for akuammicine formation; not the focal discovery gene in this paper.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Known MIA pathway gene; epidermis-enriched relative to whole leaf
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: coupled in vitro enzyme assays with GS1/GS2 and GO; prior pathway knowledge
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: folivory-associated transcriptome mining used for candidate selection context
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful upstream helper enzyme for pathway reconstruction, but not the core engineering target identified here.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SGD deglycosylates strictosidine to generate strictosidine aglycone. 2. This aglycone is the entry substrate for GS1/GS2 and GO in the newly defined akuammicine-forming system. 3. SGD serves as an upstream enabling enzyme rather than the main discovery target.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because the paper title emphasizes a three-enzyme system, but SGD is previously known and not a newly identified core causal gene in Results.

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: upstream to midstream; reductive step preceding oxidative rearrangement
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: geissoschizine (inferred transient intermediate); over-reduced side products (16R)-E-isositsirikine and (16R)-Z-isositsirikine when GO is absent
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces the 4,21-dehydrogeissoschizine isomer of strictosidine aglycone to geissoschizine; in the absence of GO, further reduction yields isositsirikine isomers.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes formation of the geissoschizine intermediate required for akuammicine production; alone mainly accumulates isositsirikine side products in vitro.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Akuammicine is a Strychnos alkaloid; preakuammicine-derived products include anticancer alkaloids vinblastine and vincristine.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: leaf epidermis-enriched pathway tissues inferred from transcript enrichment
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: transcripts enriched in epidermis relative to whole leaf
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies immediate precursor pool for GO-mediated rearrangement to preakuammicine/akuammicine
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key early branch enzyme for Strychnos scaffold formation; rate-limiting not established
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: VIGS silencing; heterologous expression in E. coli; in vitro coupled enzyme assay with SGD and GO; LC-MS; NMR structure elucidation; subcellular localization
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: candidate selection aided by transcriptomes including folivory-elevated alkaloid conditions
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Silencing caused only minor alkaloid profile changes, suggesting redundancy; accumulation of heteroyohimbines observed in GS-silenced leaves
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GS1 was identified from C. roseus transcriptomic mining as a medium-chain alcohol dehydrogenase candidate for preakuammicine pathway entry. 2. Recombinant GS1 converted strictosidine aglycone mainly to (16R)-E- and (16R)-Z-isositsirikine in vitro when assayed without GO. 3. Combined with GO and SGD, GS1 enabled formation of enantiopure akuammicine, indicating that GS1 generates a transient geissoschizine intermediate captured by GO. 4. GS1 transcripts are enriched in epidermis and the protein localizes to the nucleocytosol. 5. Weak silencing phenotype suggests partial redundancy in planta.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for combinatorial metabolic engineering of monoterpene indole alkaloids, but should be co-expressed with GO and upstream SGD because unstable intermediates require enzyme coupling.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Apparent kcat reported as 0.04 ± 0.007 s^-1 under assay conditions. GS1 product acting as GO substrate had to be generated simultaneously and was too unstable for isolation.

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: upstream to midstream; reductive step preceding oxidative rearrangement
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: geissoschizine (inferred transient intermediate); (16R)-E-isositsirikine and (16R)-Z-isositsirikine side products
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Likely performs the same reductive conversion as GS1 from strictosidine aglycone isomer to geissoschizine, with lower activity/yield in vitro; without GO accumulates isositsirikine isomers.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports akuammicine formation with GO, but less efficiently than GS1 in vitro.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Akuammicine is a Strychnos alkaloid; pathway gives access to preakuammicine-derived medicinal alkaloids.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies immediate precursor pool for GO-mediated rearrangement to akuammicine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: heterologous expression in E. coli; in vitro enzyme assay with SGD and GO; LC-MS
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Lower purification yield and lower product formation than GS1; likely co-silenced in GS1 VIGS because of 95% nucleotide identity
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GS2 is a close GS1 homolog identified in C. roseus. 2. Recombinant GS2 produced the same isositsirikine products as GS1 in lower quantity. 3. In combination with GO and SGD, GS2 also supported akuammicine formation. 4. GS2 likely contributes to functional redundancy that weakens GS1 silencing phenotypes.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Can substitute for GS1 in pathway reconstruction but appears less favorable due to lower protein yield/activity.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: GS1 silencing fragment likely co-silenced GS2 due to 95% identity.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Strychnos/preakuammicine branch from geissoschizine to preakuammicine/akuammicine scaffold
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: oxidized/rearranged intermediate leading to preakuammicine, which non-enzymatically deformylates to akuammicine
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cytochrome P450-catalyzed oxidation of geissoschizine to a rearranged intermediate proposed as rhazimal/dehydropreakuammicine or related species en route to preakuammicine; the coupled assay yields akuammicine via preakuammicine deformylation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Essential for akuammicine formation; silencing reduces downstream preakuammicine-derived alkaloids and causes accumulation of an isositsirikine-like metabolite.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Akuammicine is a Strychnos alkaloid and κ-opioid receptor agonist; preakuammicine-derived products include anticancer alkaloids vinblastine and vincristine.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: leaf epidermis-enriched pathway tissues inferred from transcript enrichment
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: transcripts enriched in epidermis relative to whole leaf
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directly forms rearranged precursor of preakuammicine/akuammicine
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: essential oxidative branch-defining step; rate-limiting not established
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: VIGS silencing in planta; heterologous expression in yeast microsomes; coupled in vitro assay with SGD and GS1/GS2; LC-MS; NMR; chiral LC-MS; formaldehyde release assay; subcellular localization
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: candidate selection aided by transcriptomes including folivory-elevated alkaloid conditions
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Silencing decreased catharanthine, vindoline and vindorosine, showing broad downstream flux consequences across preakuammicine-derived alkaloids
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP71D1V1 was identified by transcript correlation with SGD and functionally renamed geissoschizine oxidase (GO). 2. VIGS of GO reduced catharanthine, vindoline and vindorosine, consistent with a role upstream of many preakuammicine-derived alkaloids. 3. GO was absolutely required with GS1/GS2 and SGD for production of enantiopure akuammicine in vitro. 4. GO-silenced tissue accumulated a metabolite matching (16R)-E-isositsirikine, supporting coupling between GS and GO reactions. 5. GO is ER-anchored and epidermis-enriched, spatially consistent with pathway function. 6. Formaldehyde release supported formation of preakuammicine followed by non-enzymatic deformylation to akuammicine.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-value engineering target for reconstructing Strychnos/preakuammicine-derived alkaloid pathways; should be co-expressed with GS and SGD and likely requires subcellular organization compatible with ER-localized P450 function.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No direct preakuammicine peak was detected, likely because it deformylates non-enzymatically to akuammicine. GO specific activity not calculated because enzyme used as crude microsomes.
